speaker: interviewer
text: --here with survivor Frieda Aaron, on February 5, 1995. And you left off at Hof on Saale, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saale, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saale, which you said is on the Czech-German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Close-- close.

speaker: interviewer
text: --border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite on the border, but very close to the border. And there was no housing for us, so they placed us in what had been a-- like, a restaurant-- an inn-- before. And so, in fact, in the book that I am in the process of writing, I describe it in great detail. One of the rooms in that restaurant, what-- it-- the-- the-- the restaurant itself, the dining room, I should say, was transformed into a shelter. And so there were cots-- not-- not bunk beds-- but narrow cots, one next to the other. There were all sorts of people from various parts of Europe. There were gypsies and-- and-- and people from-- you know, Jewish people, and Polish people, and-- and all sorts of other people. And one day-- my two cousins were there with us. And my mother and sister, of course, were there all the time. And there we stayed for some time. It was, you know, a kind of [BACKGROUND NOISE] limbo, too, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in time are we at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is now 19-- this will be fall, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already getting quite cold, and I didn't have a coat, I remember. Anyway, I'll get to it a little bit later. And-- and my sister-- once, a-- a man picked up his left arm, and he was not dressed. And my sister noticed an SS mark. The Germans tattooed their underarms with SS. So she caught him. I mean, she-- she-- she identified him, but he ran away. All right. So there we stayed for some time. And then, ultimately, we were placed in apartments with German families. They had-- had to allow that to be, because there were no places for us. They were building a DP camp. Actually, the DP camp was there, because there was some sort of a-- I think, a-- something for soldiers, German soldiers before. So we stayed with this family. The man had been a Nazi, but denied it. But we knew, because he talked about it. He said he had no choice, you know. And it was difficult to live with them. But, you know, it's so interesting. We had two rooms. They had a large apartment. That-- I don't remember hating them. And I remember, the food was extremely scarce. I mean, you know, we were not starving anymore, because we had packages from UNRRA, you know, and packages of food from the United States. So we're not starving anymore. But they were hungry. And we used to share the food with them. And, in fact, my cousin established a relationship with that man's wife. I don't mean a-- I don't mean a sexual [LAUGHS] relationship. Just-- she was much older. And-- and then I worked for UNRRA in a DP camp. And then we separated from our cousins, because there were, you know, there was some-- there was some tension. And then ultimately, we came to the United States, and it took us 2 and 1/2 years to get affidavits. We had relatives in the United States, but it was extremely difficult to secure papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we-- we kind of languished in these awful places. I mean, it wasn't awful to live with the Germans, but it was in some way. But I remember something. And I hope I don't sound quixotic. I hope you don't think me mad, because some people may.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, distinctly. I was walking. It was a sunny day. And I hated the German with a passion that I cannot describe. And at one point, I said, I have to stop it. I cannot go on like this, because I'm becoming like them. I wasn't even thinking that it was bad for me, because I was too young to understand. And I wasn't sophisticated enough to know what hatred does to you. But I said, I'm becoming like them, and I absolutely had to stop. And I did. And it's an intellectual decision. It's not emotional. It's not spiritual. It's purely intellectual. I know it is wrong, because hatred produces what the Germans produced and other atrocities-- the Huguenots before us, you know, and-- and the Armenians, and then after us. You know, after all, after the Holocaust, everything is permissible. You know, this is a kind of paraphrase that Dostoevsky said in 'Without God, everything is permissible.' And I say, after the Holocaust, everything seems to be permissible. We thought that the Holocaust would stop. You know, it'll be a lesson. But it does not, you know, it did not. And it most certainly doesn't-- does not. And-- and-- and I stopped. And it's my-- it-- it-- I just vowed to myself that I'm not going to reduce myself to that level. As I said, it's not emotion. I cannot go to Germany. I wouldn't go to Germany. In fact, we went to Germany for three days once, because we were in Italy. My sister lived in Italy. And my husband wanted to visit his friend whom he hadn't seen in years. And, at first, I refused to go. So my husband decided to go by himself. But I couldn't let him go by himself. So I went with him, whereupon my sister and brother-in-law decided to go with us, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we drove from Italy to Munich. And there for 3-- the 2 and 1/2 days or 3 days, I cried endlessly. It was just so awfully painful. But, I-- on principle, I don't want to hate, because I know what-- what hatred does.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you something interesting that happened in Hof. We tried to secure papers. I had a very good friend. So, my mother was sort of helpless at this point. You know, she couldn't do very much. And I was all of 17. And I used to take care of all of these official things. We had to secure papers to come to the United States. We had to go to Munich, where I met my husband, as a matter of fact. And, we're in a train, my friend and I. She's a very beautiful girl. At the time, she was a girl. And we were speaking Polish, of course, and a young German man came over to us. He wanted to flirt with us. And we were very afraid to-- to admit that we were Jewish. You know, one just didn't. But ultimately, the issue came up. I don't remember precisely in what context. And we did say that we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took my hand, my left hand-- the palm. He studied it. And he says, yes. I can see that you are Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell it to my students in class. And they say, well, let me see your hand. Well, there's nothing on my hand that is different from an average person's hand. There's nothing Jewish-- particularly Jewish-- about my hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: But his-- the anti-Semitism that prevailed, you know, it was still-- it was still. And many people, when they saw us, they said, you don't look Jewish. You know, so we said, what do we look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they really believe that-- some, believe it or not, I mean, it-- it'll sound like a fairytale. But some actually believed that we had horns. Some were very-- some people were very primitive, you know, and they actually believed. And-- and they could not believe that we were Jewish. We're human, you know. So, ultimately, you know, after much struggle, we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what day did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 3, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and sister, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came from Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came from Bremerhaven. And-- but we-- we still lived in Hof. I just went to Munich very often--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to secure papers. And, in fact, the-- my sister was very clever. The ambassador, or the consulate, rather-- oops, I keep on touching this. You know, we claimed that my mother was born in Russia, because the Russian quota was lower. There were fewer Russians. So it was easier to come on the Russian quota than on the German-- on the Polish one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the consulate said to my mother-- well, she said that her sisters and brother were born in Russia. And so he says, well, you may have been born. So she was the youngest-- my mother was the youngest. You may have been born-- born elsewhere. So my sister, all of 16, or 15-- I don't think she was even 16, yet-- said, well, you don't think that my grandparents traveled from land to land to bear children. So [LAUGHS] he let us, you know, he let us go. At another time, we were interviewed by another, or-- or questioned by another American official. And he asked me if I was ever pregnant. And we were very naive and very, very innocent. And I said, oh, no, my mother was. [LAUGHS] It goes to show you the level of my sophistication at this time, because, you know, there was a kind of hiatus in our lives, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and although I understand that-- that people did make love in-- in the Warsaw ghetto, somehow we were sheltered enough. It was very important that they did, because it was an expression of-- of-- of something human. And, in fact, in Skarzysko, a man once stole into our barrack, you know. And he made love to a woman. Apparently, they had a rendezvous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was appalled in that filth-- in that-- in that-- hell-- I don't know. What word should I use? In that-- in that unspeakable, otherworldly, anti-world place, my mother scolded them. How could you do it in front of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I was 14, and my sister was 12, or something like that. Our morals would be destroyed, you know. It was so absurd. But, you know, still, she retained her-- her humanity. This is what is important. And-- but, you see, I never blamed them for it. I-- I guess I must have been appalled. I mean, I didn't watch them. [LAUGHS] But, you know, I knew, you know. And-- but, you know, somehow, I-- I think that they tried to capture of-- a moment of normal life, and maybe express love. You know, and I think he gave her a piece of bread. He had a little more bread, you know. Most people didn't make love, because the-- the libido was extremely weak. The starvation, you know, just caused total-- I'm sure, among men-- impotence. And nobody thought about-- and certainly in these awful places. So now we're in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your cousins come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they came later. They came a year or so later. Because, ultimately, we did get the papers-- affidavits-- that were actually applied for by my relatives, my mother's two sisters and a brother. And when we arrived, my husband had come before.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not married at the time. He waited at the boat, you know? Well it's not-- it was not Ellis Island, but along the Hudson River, you know, the-- 34th Street or some such. Really? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And-- and then we stayed with our relatives for a couple of weeks. And then we were pretty much on our own. And I began-- my first job-- after 10 days, I began to work. 10 days after we arrived, I began to work. And my-- my aunt scolded my mother for coming to America with unmarried girls. [LAUGHS] And I was 19. My sister was 17 and 1/2, or something like that. And we were so terrified of not being [LAUGHS] married and being an old maid. [LAUGHS] It was so absurd, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had you met Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in Germany. We met in Munich. I mentioned this before.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how did I meet him? Well, there was a club for Jewish young people, and we danced. It was called Tel Aviv. It was actually a restaurant. So this is where we met. And, in fact, we-- we-- from-- I used to go there-- not too frequently, but every so often. This friend of mine who lived in-- in Munich, she introduced me to-- to her friends. She was from Krakow. And all the people from-- were from Krakow. Saul is from-- my husband-- is from Vilna, but somehow he was integrated into that group. And so I met him there. He was a very good dancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And you stayed in touch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, yes, because I used to come very often to Munich. So we stayed in touch. And then when we-- you know, when we both arrived at separate times, we-- we met. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had not decided to get married before you left Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- we didn't have that close a relationship. And I was going out with another boy, you know. And it was, you know, tentative. I mean, I would never even discuss this issue. And there were other boys, you know? You know, we tried to resume as normal life as we could. And in-- in Hof on the Saale, there was a Jewish community center where we danced every night. And, in fact, in-- in-- in the book that I'm writing, I'm describing the room. Actually, it-- it must have been a store at one point. So-- no, I think it may have been even a bar. But I'm not sure. OK. I-- I don't want to give you too many details, because we're running out of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no-- there were electrical bulbs, but without any-- any-- any screening, you know? And there were chairs aligned against-- you know, the walls were aligned with chairs. And we danced.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the kids couldn't dance well. You know, most of-- most of us were very young. Most of the people who survived were young. Or the elderly, people even in their forties-- which isn't old-- were killed. And my sister and I, having lived in Warsaw, and having had the cousin that we had, knew how to dance. We knew how to do the swing, which is a kind of interpretation of the American swing-- [LAUGHS] a Polish interpretation of the American swing. And we taught everyone to dance. And so we became very popular, you know. And also, the fact that we're-- came from Warsaw was a boon in itself. Because the provinces were regarded with disdain in Poland, you know, which was ridiculous. And because I studied in-- in the Warsaw ghetto, and I knew some Latin, so I used to conjugate, and [LAUGHS] you know, and do the declension. So people were very impressed between that and my dancing. My reputation rested--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --on that. But I think what is so significant is that we tried-- you know, this was a very strange time. In some way-- I hope I won't shock you-- it was one of the best times in my life. Because it was a kind of vacuum. We couldn't think about the past-- it was too close and too agonizing. We couldn't think about the future, because there was no future. We could not envision a future. We did not know where we were going. We wanted to go to Israel, initially-- to Palestine, at the time. And it was impossible. Although some people did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't go to the United States. It took such a long time. So it was really a limbo, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did-- I didn't-- I don't remember feeling very much. I wanted to look nice. I wanted to have food. I wanted to have clothes, you know. But I didn't think about the future. It was-- I really don't know. There must be a term to identify the period-- you know-- a suspension, animated suspension, which is a common term. I just can't find anything better. And then-- but despite that, or maybe because of it, we tried to live as normal lives as we could. You know, my friend, the one with whom I traveled to-- Manya is her name. She played the accordion some. But she was able to get the accordion. And she played. And sometimes, I would look at her, and I would see the excruciating pain in her eyes. But nobody said a word. It was not necessary. We knew. We communicated non-verbally, you know. But so we pretended. But the pretense was very good for us. Because, in some way, it helped us to make the transition from one-- you know, from-- from-- from the horror of the-- of the Holocaust, into the horror of living where we lived in Germany. But, I mean, they're not comparable, of course. We got rations. And-- and we began to handel-- if you know what that means. You know, there was a black market. I'm not ashamed of it. I was not involved in it-- not because I had any moral scruples about it, but because I just didn't know how. But it was essential, because without it, we would have not survived. And, you know, people earn some money, bought things, sold things, you know. So it was essentially a black market. But, as I said, there was nothing else to be done. And, in fact, I'm proud of the fact that we did not depend on others to take care of us. The Americans helped, yes. They sent us food. They sent us their old clothes. And we were very grateful for it. But we also took care for ourselves, however it was possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't hurt anyone. We didn't kill anyone. Do you know that very few Jews engaged in retaliation after the war, which was widespread?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles, the Ukrainians-- well, not so much the Ukrainians-- the Poles, the Yugoslavians, all the others-- got guns. And they were shooting the Germans wherever they could. It was allowed at the time-- certainly at the beginning. And, in fact, a friend of mine told me that she was in that-- in-- in one of those groups. I don't want to mention her name. She's very famous. And she told me about it. And I was shocked that a Jewish woman would do that. And she took pride, but she was also a little bit reluctant to tell me this. So you had these ambivalent feelings. Now-- but at the same time, it's not because we're cowardly, but it was part of our culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, but at the same time, the-- the kids who were able to get to go to Palestine, the Jews who were able to finally get to Palestine with enormous difficulties, as you probably know, they were the ones who fought the 1948 War. And my very dear friend was killed during that war, who also survived Plaszow, among other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were-- it was not cowardice. It was just-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] it was a matter of ethics-- ethical values, you know, and-- and moral values, which is-- Jews don't kill, you know? So, take us to 1947 in the States when you were here with your mother and your sister, and a little worried about being a single woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all living together at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never separated. We stayed with my uncle for about a week in-- in Brooklyn. Then we moved to my aunt in the Bronx. And while we were still with my uncle, I guess we were with them more than a week. It must have been maybe two weeks, because I got my first job, sewing buttons on Grand Street, in Manhattan, you know, lower Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eastside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my boss-- I learned to sew quite well during the war. I mean, you know, it's ironic, but everything-- not everything, but often, some awful things proved to be fruitful later. Like when I was repairing the German uniforms in the Warsaw ghetto, I became a pretty good sewer-- not a seamstress, but a-- and girls, in general, knew how to sew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was sewing on buttons, and he didn't like the way I worked, because I was too slow. I didn't have experience. So he fired me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked in a greeting card factory. And I didn't have much clothes, as a matter of fact. My cousin gave me something-- a blouse that was transparent. And my-- [LAUGHS] slip sort of moved down a little bit. And so I was chided for it by a boy. Anyway, then from there, I graduated to the garment district. And I worked as a finisher. First I worked for Ann Miller. It was a rather elegant place-- courtier. And I learned to-- to do the shoulder pads. So I became an expert at that. And then I went into another factory. And I was [BACKGROUND NOISE] a-- a finisher. I was sewing, you know, pants and the lining. And then-- well, I became pregnant. And so I stopped working. I worked through my-- I don't know-- seventh, sixth month, or something like that. And then I stayed home. And I had my baby, my Fern.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- shortly after we came. We got married March 7, 1948, a few months after we came. We had-- we had a funny wedding, because we were very poor. So [LAUGHS] I'm not saying it with any sense of self pity. It's just-- it was a fact. And it was a-- an unusual wedding. You know, all our friends were there and some of our relatives. I was singing during the wedding. [LAUGHS] It was very informal. And then I-- I used to paint as a child, so I became a fashion illustrator later. But after Fern was born, I-- and then actually, after Stephanie was born, too, I decided when I was 30 years old, that my you know, life was slipping through my fingers, as it was. I wasn't doing anything that was of any significance. And I began to take-- actually, before that, I began to take courses at the New School for Social Research-- courses in philosophy. And from there, I decided-- when we moved here, actually, I decided to go to college, which was extremely difficult, because I had nothing to show that I had ever gone to school. You needed to show something. My sister did the same thing. She lived in Cleveland at the time, and she was working with a teacher in school. And she-- and my sister has a wonderful way with children. So this-- her teacher urged her to go to college. So she just went to either the college president-- it was Western--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Case Western.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- and he accepted her without anything. And she has an-- an MA in reading. And anyway, so then I decided to go to college. And I took the GRE exam. And I prepared myself, obviously. And I went into a special program. It's called the Special Baccalaureate Degree Program at Brooklyn College, from which I graduated. I'm going to both-- Phi Beta Kappa, magna cum laude, and Alpha Sigma Lambda, which was another, you know, honor. And after I stopped-- after I graduated, I began teaching at Brooklyn College. And then-- and I, of course, continued my studies. So I was-- I got two-- I got two MAs and a PhD in comparative literature. And-- and I've been teaching since that time. And I teach, among other things, the Holocaust. Virtually always, I taught the Holocaust at the graduate school, City University Graduate Center. And I taught it also at NYU-- Literary Responses to the Holocaust. And now, when I teach, I always incorporate, whenever I can, the Holocaust, you know, and especially if it's modern literature. I teach English essentially now, with an accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd like to ask you to talk a little bit about-- about your work, because-- especially because it's so directly related to the Holocaust and to your experiences in the Holocaust. But before we go onto that, we need to pause and switch the tapes one more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, actually, I tapped you because we're at--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think-- I think you should tell them what to--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have speed. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I should knock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we have speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk a little bit about your work and-- and your vision as-- as a writer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I may have indicated to you before that I have a kind of double vision, kind of bifocal vision. I see things-- I'm a woman, and I see my experiences through a girl's eyes. But, at the same time, I am a woman and an older woman. And so my interpretation of what happened to us is-- is obviously different from that of the child who was there, OK? In that sense, it's-- it's bifocal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I see things with, you know, with-- as I said, with a child's eyes. But I interpret them. But, of course, I was interpreting things, then, too. But I don't remember precisely. I just remember some of my feelings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my work essentially focuses on the literature during the Holocaust, not after the Holocaust. And while some people think that the literature that was written during the Holocaust is better, because it reflects the immediacy of experience-- while this is true, on the other hand, recalling the past and examining it, and arriving at-- at an understanding as an adult, is also important-- if I'm making myself clear. So I don't-- 'cause there's an awful lot among, you know, scholars, this-- a bit of tension about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I told you, I-- the book that I wrote is named-- the title of it is-- is It's essentially a scholarly book that-- it's a sort of exegesis of the poetry, an analysis of the poetry. I place it in-- in a historical background. Also, I talk about the forerunners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but, essentially, what I derived from it was that in-- the-- the miraculous spirit. Can you imagine people writing poetry in these--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pits of darkness? I mean, it's-- it's just-- it's just wild. You see, some Americans-- not only American scholars, but some scholars who were not there have a different perception from us. And there's a great deal of tension sometimes between the survivors who write and the survivors who think, what scholars and those who were there. And they-- it seems to me that some of them have a tendency to project from their own lives. That this to say they interpret our reality from their point of view, how-- how they think they would have behaved in a similar situation. Now, I think that this-- this moral survival was as significant as armed resistance. It's moral resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had no weapons. Nonetheless, there were uprisings in the Warsaw ghetto-- in-- not in Majdanek-- in Auschwitz, in-- in Sobibor, in Bialystok, and other places. My husband was in the underground, and so he was resisting. And he'll tell you about it himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I constantly say, maybe I already said it-- that we don't run around mad in the streets is an absolute miracle. And I'm rather proud of my people for that. And I-- and most of us had wonderful children, most of whom-- most of the children are-- work in human-- in the human services areas.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're doctors. They're psychologists, social workers, you know, because they have learned, I guess, from us, the need to help people. I don't know if it's universal, but it so happens that most of the people whom I know have children who are involved in these areas. And I'd written poetry. In fact, perhaps later, when my children come, I'd like to read a poem, when we come--

speaker: interviewer
text: If we have time--

speaker: interviewee
text: --together if we have time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --maybe we could close with that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --right now. Would you like to try to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely. Is there time? OK. In fact, I gave a reading of my poetry at the Jewish Museum. 'Under the kitchen table, my angel, you grieve your parents gone to dinner. Love transforms the white of my hair into circus purple. But now, these good tricks of grandmotherly comfort fail like yesterday's snow; your toddler anguish recoiling from the mess from fingers of smoke. So I grieve your grief in a chair, my useless fingers limp in my lap, like braids of shorn hair. The thing is, in your--'
